channel.title: Comments for page "Acquisitions - a Marshall, Carter & Dark Tale Act III"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Acquisitions - a Marshall, Carter & Dark Tale Act III"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely phenomenal in terms of pacing and character development. I was invested the whole way through- thank you for writing this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Best Tale on this site. Dread and Circus was nipping at the heels but still the tone consistency and character development was absolutely amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Years later, still by far my favorite tale on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only literature about capitalism that I enjoyed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is must be the best Series there is. All three just get better and better and it's just wonderful. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only reason this doesn't have a higher rating is because most people come to the SCP Foundation for 10 minute reads, not 3 hours, and get scared by the (excellent) wall of text.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like Acts I & II, feedback as I read.

Chapter 6:

  • Initial scene is a neat one.
  • I'm having real trouble following Edward's logic over the comb. Does he think the Horsemen are trying to set him up? That's what the grammar implies. If so, it comes out of nowhere. Does he just think that the forgery likely has some flaw? Well, I agree. Regarding 'one more theft will make him even worse~' — lol I don't believe that for a second. Also, if he believes the forgery has some flaw probably, then, uh, why is he passing on the fake still? Still, the rest of the scene is good.
  • "gestured Marshall" — gestured isn't a valid replacement for "said". Change.
  • I'm really just baffled as to why he wants to find the codex, but this scene is very good.
  • I like the use of the weather SCP — even more mysterious to not show it (and a bonus to those who know what it is, while still working if you don't).
  • Why. Why is he looking for the codex. I guess there's probably a reason, like he's gonna return it or something, but… eh. Needs at least more thought, unless I missed him thinking about it somewhere. It still seems silly.
  • Bagman. Intriguing. Also, the rest of the scene is good.

Chapter 7:

  • This conversation feels a touch odd, like it has to happen for the plot… I'm still fascinated by it, though, so not the biggest deal.
  • The dragon is disappointingly obvious as a choice [given the overuse], although it makes sense and the book is cool. Not the worst issue either.
  • I like the snatching. Neat — unexpected.
  • The reveal of the card details is very cool. I also like this scene overall.
  • The use of 'amnesiac' instead of 'amnestic/amnesic' feels especially odd since this overall has been much more realistic.
  • Had to read this a few times to be sure that Edward actually hadn't been affected by the amnesic. Might not hurt to give it another reference in the next scene (with him thinking that he still remembers everything, or thinks he does at least).
  • The Four Horsemen are at their most entertaining in this scene by far.
  • Wait, what? The Foundation didn't get the glass shard? That's… hm, not sure I believe that, but it's believable enough that I'll roll with it.
  • Next scene: Good, but "Damn you" is a bad line, a false note in an otherwise good scene. It's too standard (in fiction, not in real life).
  • Had to read the end of the next scene several times before figuring out that the silver dagger was the mirror piece — possibly tweak.
  • The transition here is well done, and unexpected. This scene is very neat. And Edward is satisfyingly clever here.
  • This is a great scene.
  • Only nitpick so far: Not sure Marshall twitching is needed.
  • Okay, done. This was really awesome and a really satisfying ending.

Overall: Yeah, this was awesome. Talk about payoff. If this story sequence were shorter, it would be among the best rated things in the entire site — not that you should, mind you. Fantastic work, much better than I would have imagined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here, have another upvote. This might be the single best thing on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, I think that this is one of the cleanest, most well-executed things I've read on this site. Your voice is crisp and authoritative enough to make what could have otherwise been a tedious first act set-up interesting in itself, and the way that grounding naturally progresses through to MCD's increasingly ominous behavior is perfectly understated, even as the doings become at times almost comically gothic. The plotting is a bit stock and predictable from the outset, but that's never really a problem; you pace it well enough that it never gets stale, and that stock narrative, when framed through Gradley's uneasy rationalization of MCD as just another criminal rich boy's club, actually ends up producing good tension in itself. Which brings me to the portrayal of MCD, which I love. It really feels organic, here, not the hyperbolic nightmare market it's usually described as but a natural part of the landscape—which, since it of course still is a hyperbolic nightmare market, makes for a very interesting reflection on that landscape. I got a very American Psycho vibe from the whole thing, not least with the specificity of detail when discussing the cuisine and the cars everybody was driving. Some seven-paragraph descriptions of what kinds of suits people were wearing would've nailed that home, but, admittedly, maybe not done wonders for the flow.

This is a damn fine piece of work, dude, and you should be seriously proud of it. Great stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same goes for me. I'm glad I read this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. Kept me hooked from start to finish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers! The ending did give me some grief - I did consider a fight sequence between the Bagman and the MTF, but it seemed tacked-on and out of true with the rest of the Tale, which as mentioned in the Author's Notes isn't exactly high-octane. A trade rather than a fight also seemed more inkeeping with the themes of the piece. If I were to go back and revisit the piece I would look at expanding 'Castle' and further developing what we see of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending falls a little flat, I think, but other than that this is amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fantastic tale, which, I think, uses every SCP in an appropriate and effective way. Heartily upvoted. Please do more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finished it up. Wonderful, and though the last part changes the pace a little and ends up being action movie fare, it'd be an action movie even a cheapskate like me would pay to see.
Very very nice job with the feeding to Carter's chair - I love it when someone makes a threat he doesn't understand, and it works.

"You know what I think?" said Edward. "I think you don't kill, or despoil, or maim, or kidnap—unless it profits you. The fact of the matter is, if I don't care what you do to my mother, to Maria, they are safe. So go ahead—except you can't, because it would mean nothing. I tried to explain to David—the ideal of capitalism. Everyone working in their self-interest—and only in their self-interest—is the optimum solution to the societal puzzle. I still believe that; I've just learned to expand my definition of self-interest." He raised his voice. "I won't help you launder drugs and arms dealers' money. I won't help you steal antiques and sell them to dictators. I won't do those things because it is in my self-interest to live in a stable, lawful society, where I can trade in fair competition with others."

This particular speech made me giggle incessantly, especially knowing that Ayn Rand was a bit of an inspiration.

As an aside, the question I asked you in the chat was answered by the text itself - what with the second person having an active interest for the other partner to not outgrow him, and him not performing well in acquisitions, it would make sense to go with the first's wishes, for a price.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally had the chance to finish this last section. This vision of MC&D is richer than any I've yet seen on the site. Absolutely amazing work Sreagan, you've permanently impacted my headcanon with all of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: finished, and blown away. i cant recall having read a tale on here like this. you deserve all the upvotes i can give. no disappointment whatsoever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…SRegan…that was a damned good read. Excellent writing, great plot, and interesting characters. There were a few grammar issues, especially towards the end, but none going so far as to actually disrupt my enjoyment of the work.

You did a great job with the MC&D GoI, as with the Foundation. Very interesting expansion of the universe, and a cracking good tale.
